html:
XIII

EdouardвАЩs Journal: First Visit to La P√©rouse

On tire peu de service des vieillards.

Vauvenargues

Nov. 8th.вБ†вАФOld Monsieur and Madame de la P√©rouse have changed houses again. Their new apartment, which I had never seen so far, is an entresol in the part of the Faubourg St.¬†Honor√© which makes a little recess before it cuts across the Boulevard Haussmann. I rang the bell. La P√©rouse opened the door. He was in his shirt sleeves and was wearing a sort of yellowish whitish nightcap on his head, which I finally made out to be an old stocking (Madame de La P√©rouseвАЩs no doubt) tied in a knot, so that the foot dangled on his cheek like a tassel. He was holding a bent poker in his hand. I had evidently caught him at some domestic job, and as he seemed rather confused:

вАЬWould you like me to come back later?вАЭ I asked.

вАЬNo, no.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Come in here.вАЭ And he pushed me into a long, narrow room with two windows looking on to the street, just on a level with the street lamp. вАЬI was expecting a pupil at this very momentвАЭ (it was six oвАЩclock); вАЬbut she has telegraphed to say she canвАЩt come. I am so glad to see you.вАЭ

He laid his poker down on a small table, and, as though apologizing for his appearance:

вАЬMadame de La P√©rouseвАЩs maidservant has let the stove go out. She only comes in the morning; IвАЩve been obliged to empty it.вАЭ

вАЬShall I help you light it?вАЭ

вАЬNo, no; itвАЩs dirty work.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Will you excuse me while I go and put my coat on?вАЭ

He trotted out of the room and came back almost immediately dressed in an alpaca coat, with its buttons torn off, its elbows in holes, and its general appearance so threadbare, that one wouldnвАЩt have dared give it to a beggar. We sat down.

вАЬYou think IвАЩm changed, donвАЩt you?вАЭ

I wanted to protest, but could hardly find anything to say, I was so painfully affected by the harassed expression of his face, which had once been so beautiful. He went on:

вАЬYes, IвАЩve grown very old lately. IвАЩm beginning to lose my memory. When I want to go over one of BachвАЩs fugues, I am obliged to refer to the book.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬThere are many young people who would be glad to have a memory like yours.вАЭ

He replied with a shrug: вАЬOh, itвАЩs not only my memory thatвАЩs failing. For instance, I think I still walk pretty quickly; but all the same everybody in the street passes me.вАЭ

вАЬOh,вАЭ said I, вАЬpeople walk much quicker nowadays.вАЭ

вАЬYes, donвАЩt they?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ItвАЩs the same with my lessonsвБ†вАФmy pupils think that my teaching keeps them back; they want to go quicker than I do. IвАЩm losing them.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ EveryoneвАЩs in a hurry nowadays.вАЭ

He added in a whisper so low that I could hardly hear him: вАЬIвАЩve scarcely any left.вАЭ

I felt that he was in such great distress that I didnвАЩt dare question him.

вАЬMadame de La P√©rouse wonвАЩt understand. She says I donвАЩt set about it in the right wayвБ†вАФthat I donвАЩt do anything to keep them and still less to get new ones.вАЭ

вАЬThe pupil you were expecting just now.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ I asked awkwardly.

вАЬOh, she! IвАЩm preparing her for the Conservatoire. She comes here to practise every day.вАЭ

вАЬWhich means she doesnвАЩt pay you.вАЭ

вАЬMadame de La P√©rouse is always reproaching me with it. She canвАЩt understand that those are the only lessons that interest me; yes, the only lessons I really care aboutвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ giving. I have taken to reflecting a great deal lately. Here! thereвАЩs something I should like to ask you. Why is it there is so little about old people in books?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I suppose itвАЩs because old people arenвАЩt able to write themselves and young ones donвАЩt take any interest in them. No oneвАЩs interested in an old man.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And yet there are a great many curious things that might be said about them. For instance: there are certain acts in my past life which IвАЩm only just beginning to understand. Yes, IвАЩm just beginning to understand that they havenвАЩt at all the meaning I attached to them in the old days when I did them.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩve only just begun to understand that I have been a dupe during the whole of my life. Madame de La P√©rouse has fooled me; my son has fooled me; everybody has fooled me; God has fooled me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

The evening was closing in. I could hardly make out my old masterвАЩs features; but suddenly the light of the street lamp flashed out and showed me his cheeks glittering with tears. I looked anxiously at first at an odd mark on his temple, like a dint, like a hole; but as he moved a little, the spot changed places and I saw that it was only a shadow cast by a knob of the balustrade. I put my hand on his scraggy arm; he shivered.

вАЬYouвАЩll catch cold,вАЭ I said. вАЬReally, shanвАЩt we light the fire?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Come along.вАЭ

вАЬNo, no; one must harden oneself.вАЭ

вАЬWhat? Stoicism?вАЭ

вАЬYes, a little. ItвАЩs because my throat was delicate that I never would wear a scarf. I have always struggled with myself.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs all very well as long as one is victorious; but if oneвАЩs body gives way.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬThat would be the real victory.вАЭ

He let go my hand and went on: вАЬI was afraid you would go away without coming to see me.вАЭ

вАЬGo where?вАЭ I asked.

вАЬI donвАЩt know. You travel so much. ThereвАЩs something I wanted to say to you.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I expect to be going away myself soon.вАЭ

вАЬWhat! are you thinking of travelling?вАЭ I asked clumsily, pretending not to understand him, notwithstanding the mysterious solemnity of his voice. He shook his head.

вАЬYou know very well what I mean.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Yes, yes. I know it will soon be time. I am beginning to earn less than my keep; and I canвАЩt endure it. ThereвАЩs a certain point beyond which I have promised myself not to go.вАЭ

He spoke in an emotional tone which alarmed me.

вАЬDo you think it is wrong? I have never been able to understand why it was forbidden by religion. I have reflected a great deal latterly. When I was young, I led a very austere life; I used to congratulate myself on my force of character every time I refused a solicitation in the street. I didnвАЩt understand, that when I thought I was freeing myself, in reality I was becoming more and more the slave of my own pride. Every one of these triumphs over myself was another turn of the key in the door of my prison. ThatвАЩs what I meant just now by saying that God had fooled me. He made me take my pride for virtue. He was laughing at me. It amuses him. I think he plays with us as a cat does with a mouse. He sends us temptations which he knows we shanвАЩt be able to resist; but when we do resist he revenges himself still worse. Why does he hate us so? And why.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But IвАЩm boring you with these old manвАЩs questions.вАЭ

He took his head in his hands like a moping child and remained silent so long that I began to wonder whether he had not forgotten my presence. I sat motionless in front of him, afraid of disturbing his meditations. Notwithstanding the noise of the street which was so close, the calm of the little room seemed to me extraordinary, and notwithstanding the glimmer of the street lamp, which shed its fantastic light upon us from down below, like footlights at the theatre, the shadow on each side of the window seemed to broaden, and the darkness round us to thicken, as in icy weather the water of a quiet pool thickens into immobilityвБ†вАФtill my heart itself thickened into ice too. At last, shaking myself free from the clutch that held me, I breathed loudly and, preparatory to taking my leave, I asked out of politeness and in order to break the spell:

вАЬHow is Madame de La P√©rouse?вАЭ

The old man seemed to wake up out of a dream. He repeated:

вАЬMadame de La P√©rouseвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАК?вАЭ interrogatively, as if the words were syllables which had lost all meaning for him; then he suddenly leant towards me:

вАЬMadame de La P√©rouse is in a terrible stateвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ most painful to me.вАЭ

вАЬWhat kind of state?вАЭ I asked.

вАЬOh, no kind,вАЭ he said, shrugging his shoulders, as if there were nothing to explain. вАЬShe is completely out of her mind. She doesnвАЩt know what to be up to next.вАЭ

I had long suspected that the old couple were in profound disagreement, but without any hope of knowing anything more definite.

вАЬMy poor friend,вАЭ I said pityingly, вАЬand since when?вАЭ

He reflected a moment, as if he had not understood my question.

вАЬOh, for a long timeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ever since IвАЩve known her.вАЭ Then, correcting himself almost immediately: вАЬNo; in reality it was over my sonвАЩs bringing up that things went wrong.вАЭ

I made a gesture of surprise, for I had always thought that the La P√©rouses had no children. He raised his head, which he had been holding in his hands, and went on more calmly:

вАЬI never mentioned my son to you, eh?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, IвАЩll tell you everything. You must know all about it now. ThereвАЩs no one else I can tell.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Yes, it was over my sonвАЩs bringing up. As you see, itвАЩs a long time ago. The first years of our married life had been delightful. I was very pure when I married Madame de La P√©rouse. I loved her with innocenceвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ yes, thatвАЩs the best word for it, and I refused to allow that she had any faults. But we hadnвАЩt the same ideas about bringing up children. Every time that I wanted to reprove my son, Madame de La P√©rouse took his side against me; according to her, he was to be allowed to do anything he liked. They were in league together against me. She taught him to lie.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ When he was barely twenty he took a mistress. She was a pupil of mineвБ†вАФa Russian girl, with a great talent for music, to whom I was very much attached. Madame de La P√©rouse knew all about it; but of course, as usual, everything was kept from me. And of course I didnвАЩt notice she was going to have a baby. Not a thingвБ†вАФI tell you; I never suspected a thing. One fine day, I am informed that my pupil is unwell, that she wonвАЩt be able to come for some time. When I speak about going to see her, I am told that she has changed her addressвБ†вАФthat she is travelling.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It was not till long after that I learnt that she had gone to Poland for her confinement. My son joined her there.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ They lived together for several years, but he died before marrying her.вАЭ

вАЬAndвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ she? did you ever see her again?вАЭ

He seemed to be butting with his head against some obstacle:

вАЬI couldnвАЩt forgive her for deceiving me. Madame de La P√©rouse still corresponds with her. When I learnt she was in great poverty, I sent her some money for the childвАЩs sake. But Madame de La P√©rouse knows nothing about that. No more does sheвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ she doesnвАЩt know the money came from me.вАЭ

вАЬAnd your grandson?вАЭ

A strange smile flitted over his face; he got up.

вАЬWait a moment. IвАЩll show you his photograph.вАЭ And again he trotted quickly out of the room, poking his head out in front of him. When he came back, his fingers trembled as he looked for the picture in a large letter-case. He held it towards me and, bending forward, whispered in a low voice:

вАЬI took it from Madame de La P√©rouse without her noticing. She thinks she has lost it.вАЭ

вАЬHow old is he?вАЭ I asked.

вАЬThirteen. He looks older, doesnвАЩt he? He is very delicate.вАЭ

His eyes filled with tears once more; he held out his hand for the photograph, as if he were anxious to get it back again as quickly as possible. I leant forward to look at it in the dim light of the street lamp; I thought the child was like him; I recognized old La P√©rouseвАЩs high, prominent forehead and dreamy eyes. I thought I should please him by saying so; he protested:

вАЬNo, no; itвАЩs my brother heвАЩs likeвБ†вАФa brother I lost.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

The child was oddly dressed in a Russian embroidered blouse.

вАЬWhere does he live?вАЭ

вАЬHow can I tell?вАЭ cried La P√©rouse, in a kind of despair. вАЬThey keep everything from me, I tell you.вАЭ

He had taken the photograph, and after having looked at it a moment, he put it back in the letter-case, which he slipped into his pocket.

вАЬWhen his mother comes to Paris, she only sees Madame de La P√©rouse; if I question her, she always answers: вАШYou had better ask her yourself.вАЩ She says that, but at heart she would hate me to see her. She has always been jealous. She has always tried to take away everything I care for.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Little Boris is being educated in PolandвБ†вАФat Warsaw, I believe. But he often travels with his mother.вАЭ Then, in great excitement: вАЬOh, would you have thought it possible to love someone one has never seen?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, this child is what I care for most in the world.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And he doesnвАЩt know!вАЭ

His words were broken by great sobs. He rose from his chair and threw himselfвБ†вАФfell almostвБ†вАФinto my arms. I would have done anything to give him some comfortвБ†вАФbut what could I do? I got up, for I felt his poor shrunken form slipping to the ground and I thought he was going to fall on his knees. I held him up, embraced him, rocked him like a child. He mastered himself. Madame de La P√©rouse was calling in the next room.

вАЬSheвАЩs coming.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You donвАЩt want to see her, do you?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Besides, sheвАЩs stone deaf. Go quickly.вАЭ And as he saw me out on to the landing:

вАЬDonвАЩt be too long without coming again.вАЭ (There was entreaty in his voice.) вАЬGoodbye; goodbye.вАЭ

Nov. 9th.вБ†вАФThere is a kind of tragedy, it seems to me, which has hitherto almost entirely eluded literature. The novel has dealt with the contrariness of fate, good or evil fortune, social relationships, the conflicts of passions and of charactersвБ†вАФbut not with the very essence of manвАЩs being.

And yet, the whole effect of Christianity was to transfer the drama on to the moral plane. But properly speaking there are no Christian novels. There are novels whose purpose is edification; but that has nothing to do with what I mean. Moral tragedyвБ†вАФthe tragedy, for instance, which gives such terrific meaning to the Gospel text: вАЬIf the salt have lost his flavour wherewith shall it be salted?вАЭвБ†вАФthat is the tragedy with which I am concerned.

Nov. 10th.вБ†вАФOlivierвАЩs examination is coming on shortly. Pauline wants him to try for the √Йcole Normale afterwards. His career is all mapped out.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If only he had no parents, no connections! I would have made him my secretary. But the thought of me never occurs to him; he has not even noticed my interest in him, and I should embarrass him if I showed it. It is because I donвАЩt want to embarrass him that I affect a kind of indifference in his presence, a kind of detachment. It is only when he does not see me that I dare look my full at him. Sometimes I follow him in the street without his knowing it. Yesterday I was walking behind him in this way, when he turned suddenly round before I had time to hide.

вАЬWhere are you off to in such a hurry?вАЭ I asked him.

вАЬOh, nowhere particular. I always seem most in a hurry when I have nothing to do.вАЭ

We took a few steps together, but without finding anything to say to each other. He was certainly put out at having been met.

Nov. 12th.вБ†вАФHe has parents, an elder brother, school friends.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I keep repeating this to myself all day longвБ†вАФand that there is no room for me. I should no doubt be able to make up anything that might be lacking to him, but nothing is. He needs nothing; and if his sweetness delights me, there is nothing in it that allows me for a moment to deceive myself.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh, foolish words, which I write in spite of myself and which discover the duplicity of my heart.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I am leaving for London tomorrow. I have suddenly made up my mind to go away. It is time.

To go away because one is too anxious to stay!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A certain love of the arduousвБ†вАФa horror of indulgence (towards oneself, I mean) is perhaps the part of my Puritan upbringing which I find it hardest to free myself from.

Yesterday, at SmithвАЩs, bought a copybook (English already) in which to continue my diary. I will write nothing more in this one. A new copybook!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

Ah! if it were myself I could leave behind!
